hcd_data.architectural_style: Converted House-form, Row (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Second Empire and other Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a row, the properties have evolved over time while retaining original building features, including shared mansard roofline, dormer windows, asymmetrical facades, first storey bay windows, and segmentally arched openings. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintain the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.

context.street_character: One-way EB | 40 km/h | Sub-standard sidewalks | Mixed commercial-residential

assessment.signage: Fun [partially visible billboard advertisement on roofline]; Armacy [storefront signage on right unit]

photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Painted stucco in fair condition; some wear and aging visible; second floor balcony with metal railing
photo_observations.window_details: Two windows at second floor; ground floor is commercial storefront with no visible traditional windows
photo_observations.door_details: Single grey door at ground level with arched transom above; commercial entry
photo_observations.cornice_details: Simple parapet/cornice line at roofline; minimal projection
photo_observations.decorative_details: Metal second-floor balcony railing; painted stucco facade
photo_observations.storefront_description: Ground floor commercial with storefront glazing; Baru Baron business
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Entire facade painted salmon-pink stucco over original brick; ground floor heavily modified for commercial use; second-floor balcony added
photo_observations.notes: Part of a row of converted house-form buildings; significant facade alteration from original Victorian character

storefront_description: Ground floor commercial with storefront glazing; Baru Baron business
notes: Part of a row of converted house-form buildings; significant facade alteration from original Victorian character

facade_detail.composition: The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a flat, consistent with its converted house-form, row typology.
facade_detail.opening_rhythm: The facade is organized as a storefront base with 2 upper-window bays, using mixed, and 1 principal entrance.
facade_detail.heritage_summary: Constructed as a row, the properties have evolved over time while retaining original building features, including shared mansard roofline, dormer windows, asymmetrical facades, first storey bay windows, and segmentally arched openings. Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Second Empire and other Victorian architectural styles.

facade_description: Two-storey converted row house (pre-1889) with flat roof and parapet, 5.2 m wide. Salmon-pink painted stucco over original masonry, heavily altered from its Victorian character. Party walls on both sides. Active commercial ground floor (Baru Baron) with 1 dwelling unit above. Second-floor projecting metal balcony and simple cornice at roofline.
heritage_summary: Constructed as a row, the properties have evolved over time while retaining original building features, including shared mansard roofline, dormer windows, asymmetrical facades, first storey bay windows, and segmentally arched openings. Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Second Empire and other Victorian architectural styles.
